findings.summary: Three settings persistence abstractions diverge across shared and tools layers
evidence: Settings persistence currently routes through useToolSettings, useAutoSaveSettings, and usePersistentToolState with overlapping responsibilities.
Pattern detector flags competing tool_settings approaches in both src/shared and src/tools.
findings.suggestion: Converge new feature work on useAutoSaveSettings as the primary abstraction, keep usePersistentToolState as an adapter-only API, and document a migration path for legacy callers.
findings.reasoning: Overlapping abstractions increase onboarding cost, encourage inconsistent save semantics, and raise cross-module coupling risk.

findings.summary: A small set of shared modules carries disproportionate fan-in and broad responsibilities
evidence: handleError.ts and shots.ts sit at the top of import fan-in and act as broad dependency hubs.
These modules blend multiple concerns (normalization + side effects, and many domain type families) which expands blast radius per change.
findings.suggestion: Split high-fan-in modules by responsibility boundaries: isolate toast/log presentation from core error normalization, and partition shot-related types into smaller domain-focused files.
findings.reasoning: High fan-in modules become architectural choke points, slowing changes and reducing local reasoning quality.

findings.summary: Generation and settings paths still mix null-return, throw, and heterogeneous async contracts
evidence: Holistic API snapshot flags sync/async contract mixing across core generation and transform pathways.
Error strategy profile shows widespread mixture of try/catch, throws, and nullable-return conventions across hooks/lib/services.
findings.suggestion: Adopt one explicit async service contract at module boundaries (AppError throws or a typed Result envelope), then align call sites incrementally by area.
findings.reasoning: Inconsistent contracts force defensive branching in callers and reduce predictability of failure handling.

findings.summary: Legacy migration markers remain concentrated in segment and travel settings pathways
evidence: Migration signals include concentrated deprecated markers across segment settings and travel-between-images types.
Multiple compatibility layers still coexist in runtime settings/task shape handling.
findings.suggestion: Run a bounded migration-closure pass: retire deprecated aliases after converting active call paths, and keep one compatibility shim with an explicit removal milestone.
findings.reasoning: Long-lived migration residue increases cognitive load and can produce divergent behavior between old and new data shapes.
